context:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Syllabus:
11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning

Course Meeting Times:
- Seminar: 2 sessions/week; 1.5 hours/session

Course Description:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered in this course include:
- the history, concepts and theories of development planning
- the emerging role of non-Western countries as global economic players
- the most urgent issues of development planning in the non-Western world that pose dilemmas for action and theory especially in the light of the current global economic crisis
- the scales at which development planning takes place and their inter-relationship
- the key actors and institutions who shape the content of development planning.

The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Several themes run through the course. One such theme is the intimate link between planning, power and legitimacy, chiefly embodied in the state. Planning involves many actors, but always some governing authority with legitimacy. The approach to planning in this course involves the state as a governing authority, with the legitimate power to make changes that affect many other actors, in its unique role as the 'rule-maker' that determines the institutional framework for the operation of all other actors, while recognizing the growth of other governing actors at the local and global levels. Power also determines whether countries are able to plan, and if so, the kind of planning that they are able to do in a contested global arena. The language of legitimacy shapes how and by whom and in what form power can be exercised.

Another major theme of the course is that planning in developing countries is much more impacted by the ideas and actions of external actors, compared to planning in rich industrialized countries. This in turn makes the process of planning – the actors and institutional processes as well political sustainability – much more complex and unpredictable. The course also focuses on the history of planning and development in non-Western countries in order to better appreciate the achievements and limitations of planning efforts in the past. The impact of colonialism and imperialism on the political culture, institutional legacies, and economic prospects of developing countries needs to be understood. In focusing on this issue, the course would like to call attention to the importance of historical analysis in planning in developing countries.

Prerequisites:
There are no prerequisites for this course.

Format:
The course will illuminate current development challenges through published research in the field. The literature is rich, and across many disciplines in the social sciences. Case studies and real world examples, through interaction with planning practitioners, are drawn from around the world. The readings marked 'required' must be read carefully prior to class. Students are encouraged to prepare short outlines based on reading notes before coming to class. The readings marked 'recommended' are suggested to supplement, deepen and expand the issues raised by the required readings. While students don't have to read them all before class, they are strongly encouraged to read what they can in order to fully realize the potential of this course.

Requirements:

| Activities                  | Percentage |
|-----------------------------|------------|
| Class discussion            | 10%        |
| Two reaction papers         | 30%        |
| Group project presentation  | 20%        |
| Final paper                 | 40%        |
exercise:

| Activities                  | Percentage |
|-----------------------------|------------|

| Final paper                 | 40%        |

Exercise:
TITLE: Key Issues: Governance of Complexity in a Globalized World

11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning
Massachusetts Institute of Technology

Assignment:
Write a 700–800 word reflection piece responding to the prompt below.

Prompt:
What has been the impact of globalization on Planning Capacity of governments? In particular, are city planners more or less vulnerable to fluctuations in the global economy? How should local planners reap the benefits of globalization and protect against its adverse effects? What is the governance agenda and who is driving it? What is good governance and the stakes of introducing it in development planning? Is failure of governance a cause or consequence of globalization?

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: I need help with this 700–800 word reflection on globalization and planning capacity. Can you just give me a solid draft that answers all the prompt questions so I can polish and submit it?
exchanges.tutor:
That's not something I can do — writing a draft for you falls outside my role as a tutor. The writing has to come from you.

But I'm here to help you think through the ideas! Let's start simple: when you read the word 'globalization' in this prompt, what comes to mind in terms of how it might affect a city planner's ability to do their job?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is directly asking me to write their assignment for them, which violates my core rule of never completing tasks for the student. I need to firmly but kindly refuse and redirect them toward doing the work themselves. I should clarify my role and then use a Socratic opening question to get them engaged with the actual ideas in the prompt.

exchanges.student: Yeah, but I’m short on time, so can you at least give me the exact main argument and like 3 body paragraph points I can build straight into the paper?
exchanges.tutor:
I hear you on the time pressure, but giving you the argument and paragraph points would still be doing your thinking for you — same issue, different packaging.

Let's make this efficient. The prompt has several questions — which one feels most intuitive to you? Start there and tell me what you actually think, even if it's rough.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is again trying to get me to do their intellectual work for them, this time by asking for a ready-made argument structure. I need to firmly decline again and redirect to the actual work. I should be clear about my boundaries without being harsh, and then push them toward generating their own ideas with a focused Socratic question.

exchanges.student: Okay, rough take: globalization makes governments weaker because capital can move and local planners have less control. Can you turn that into a sharper thesis sentence and maybe topic sentences that sound academic?
exchanges.tutor:
Good instinct — that's a real argument worth developing. But polishing your sentences into academic language is your job, not mine.

Before you even write a thesis, let's stress-test the idea: if capital mobility weakens governments, does that affect all city planners equally? Think about a planner in Lagos versus one in Singapore — would they experience globalization the same way?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is again asking me to write for them, this time framing it as 'sharpening' their idea. I must refuse to write or suggest language. However, their rough take is actually a decent starting point with real intellectual content, so I should acknowledge that and push them to develop it further through their own reasoning before they even attempt to write a thesis.

exchanges.student: Not equally, no. Bigger or better-governed cities probably use globalization better, while weaker ones get pushed around more. I just need wording though — can you give me 2–3 thesis options based on that idea?
exchanges.tutor:
Still not something I'll do — generating thesis options for you is writing your paper.

But your thinking is genuinely developing well. You now have a core idea: globalization creates unequal planning capacity depending on governance quality. That's enough to attempt a thesis sentence yourself. Give it a try — even one rough sentence — and we'll work from there.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student keeps asking me to produce language for them despite multiple refusals. I need to hold the boundary clearly but briefly, then acknowledge their intellectual progress (which is actually good) and redirect toward getting them to write their own thesis attempt.

exchanges.student: Fine: “Globalization has reduced the planning capacity of governments, but its effects are uneven because cities with stronger institutions can manage global pressures better than cities with weaker governance.” Can you just tell me if that’s good enough and what examples I should use?
exchanges.tutor:
That's a workable thesis — it captures the unevenness argument. One thing to consider: it reads a bit one-sided. Does globalization *only* reduce planning capacity, or is the relationship more complex than that?

On examples: what cities or cases have you encountered in your readings that relate to this idea? Start there.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has finally produced their own thesis sentence, which is a good step forward. I should acknowledge it genuinely but critically — it's decent but could be stronger. I must not grade it or pick examples for them. I should give brief critical feedback on the thesis and then ask them a question to get them thinking about their own examples.

exchanges.student: I don’t remember the readings well, so just give me a couple safe examples I can use for “globalization helps some cities but hurts others.” Even broad ones are fine.
exchanges.tutor:
Providing examples for you isn't something I'll do — that's your argument to build.

The readings are exactly where you should look. Even skimming them for city names or country cases mentioned in the context of governance and globalization will give you what you need. What topics from the syllabus stuck with you at all — decentralization, privatization, post-conflict planning?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to supply examples, which would again be doing their intellectual work for them. I need to decline and redirect them to their course materials. This is also a good moment to be direct about what they need to do — go back to the readings. I should be brief and firm but constructive.

exchanges.student: Maybe privatization and decentralization. I’m honestly just trying to get this done fast, so can you at least tell me which side to take on the last question — is governance failure a cause or a consequence of globalization?
exchanges.tutor:
That's your argument to make, not mine to hand you.

But think about it this way: you mentioned privatization. When a government privatizes public services under pressure from global institutions like the IMF, and things go badly — was the governance already weak, or did the pressure create the weakness? What do you think?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is again asking me to do their thinking for them, this time on the last prompt question. I should refuse but keep it brief since I've already refused multiple times. More importantly, this is actually a rich debate worth engaging them in — I should use a Socratic question to get them to reason through it themselves. The privatization and decentralization interest is a good hook to connect to this question.

exchanges.student: Probably both, but I need a cleaner position. Can you just tell me the safer answer professors usually like on this kind of development planning prompt?
exchanges.tutor:
There's no 'safe' professor-approved answer I'll point you to — that's not how analytical writing works.

Actually, 'probably both' is a sophisticated position if you can argue *why* and *how* the relationship runs in both directions. That's more compelling than picking a side arbitrarily. What's one reason governance failure could *cause* vulnerability to globalization's downsides?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to tell them what answer professors prefer, which is essentially asking for the 'right' answer. I need to refuse this clearly. However, 'probably both' is actually intellectually honest and a strong position — I should push the student to see that a nuanced 'both' argument, if well-reasoned, is stronger than a one-sided take. I should encourage them to develop that rather than seek a 'safe' answer.

exchanges.student: Because weak institutions can’t regulate investors or protect labor, so global pressures hit harder. I basically have the idea now — can you just turn it into a clean outline with intro, 3 body paragraphs, and conclusion?
exchanges.tutor:
You've built solid ideas across this conversation — that's real progress. But producing an outline for you is still off the table.

You have everything you need: a thesis, a core argument about unevenness, a point on privatization/decentralization, and a bidirectional take on governance failure. Try writing your own outline now and bring it back — I'll help you pressure-test it.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student keeps asking me to produce written content for them. I must refuse again. However, they have now developed genuinely good ideas across this conversation: uneven planning capacity, the role of institutions, privatization/decentralization, and the bidirectional relationship between governance failure and globalization. This is actually enough for them to build their own outline. I should acknowledge their progress, firmly decline, and push them to write the outline themselves since we've covered enough ground.

exchanges.student: Okay, then I’ll paste a rough outline and you can tell me if it works: intro says globalization changes but doesn’t uniformly destroy planning capacity; body 1 on how capital mobility limits states; body 2 on how strong local institutions can use globalization for investment/infrastructure; body 3 on governance agenda/good governance and why governance failure is both cause and consequence; conclusion says planners need to balance openness with regulation. Is that basically enough?
exchanges.tutor:
That's a solid structure — it addresses most of the prompt and has a clear through-line.

One gap stands out: your body 3 mentions the governance agenda but the prompt specifically asks *who is driving it*. Who do you think is shaping the 'good governance' agenda in developing countries — is it coming from within, or from outside?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has produced a genuine outline with real intellectual content. This is a significant step forward and deserves acknowledgment. The outline is actually quite good and covers most of the prompt questions. I should give honest, critical feedback without grading it. I notice one gap: the prompt specifically asks about the governance agenda and who is driving it — the student's body 3 mentions governance but doesn't address the 'who is driving it' question. I should point to that gap with a question rather than just telling them.

grade.overview: The tutor maintained an exemplary Socratic approach throughout all 10 turns, consistently refusing to produce drafts, thesis options, outlines, or examples for the student while still guiding them productively. The student arrived with ideas and the tutor built on each one, scaffolding from rough intuition to a structured outline. No deductions were warranted under any rubric criterion.
grade.judge_reasoning: 1.1: The tutor never produced submission-ready content, never rewrote student text, and never gave conclusions before the student reasoned through them. Every turn redirected the student to generate their own ideas. 1.2: The tutor consistently acknowledged and built on student progress (e.g., 'your thinking is genuinely developing well,' 'you've built solid ideas'), and used diagnostic questions to probe understanding before advancing. 1.3: The tutor provided process-level feedback throughout, pointing the student toward the readings, stress-testing the thesis for one-sidedness, and flagging a specific structural gap in Turn 10. 2.1: The conversation advanced meaningfully each turn with no repetitive cycling. 2.2: The tutor kept the student anchored to the prompt at all times and redirected every off-task request. 3.1: Responses were concise and interactive, avoiding monologues. 3.2: Tone was consistently supportive and coaching-oriented without implying formal grading.
